title: Word ladder I (unweighted shortest path in implicit graph).
description: Word ladder I (unweighted shortest path in implicit graph).

examples.explanation:
Start: hit (path length 1)
DFS hit → hot (length 2, visited: hit,hot)
  DFS hot → dot (length 3, visited: hit,hot,dot)
    DFS dot → dog (length 4, visited: hit,hot,dot,dog)
      DFS dog → cog (length 5, visited: hit,hot,dot,dog,cog) → Found! Update min_length=5
      Backtrack: remove cog from visited
    Backtrack: remove dog from visited
    DFS dot → lot (length 4, visited: hit,hot,dot,lot)
      DFS lot → log (length 5, visited: hit,hot,dot,lot,log)
        DFS log → cog (length 6, visited: hit,hot,dot,lot,log,cog) → Found! min_length remains 5
        Backtrack: remove cog
      Backtrack: remove log
    Backtrack: remove lot
  Backtrack: remove dot
  DFS hot → lot (length 3, visited: hit,hot,lot)
    DFS lot → dot (length 4, visited: hit,hot,lot,dot) → dot already visited in this path, skip
    DFS lot → log (length 4, visited: hit,hot,lot,log)
      DFS log → dog (length 5, visited: hit,hot,lot,log,dog)
        DFS dog → cog (length 6, visited: hit,hot,lot,log,dog,cog) → Found! min_length remains 5
        Backtrack: remove cog
      Backtrack: remove dog
      DFS log → cog (length 5, visited: hit,hot,lot,log,cog) → Found! min_length remains 5
      Backtrack: remove cog
    Backtrack: remove log
  Backtrack: remove lot
Backtrack: remove hot
Return min_length=5

Start: a (path length 1)
DFS a → b (length 2, visited: a,b)
  DFS b → c (length 3, visited: a,b,c) → Found! Update min_length=3
  Backtrack: remove c
Backtrack: remove b
DFS a → c (length 2, visited: a,c) → Found! Update min_length=2
Backtrack: remove c
Return min_length=2

approaches.name: Brute Force DFS O(b^d)
approaches.summary: Explore all possible sequences from the start word to the end word using depth-first search, checking each transformation step by step. This is inefficient due to exponential growth in possibilities but conceptually simple.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force DFS for Word Ladder I
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of DFS exploring all transformation sequences from start to end word.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete Word Ladder I problem solved using brute force DFS. The intuition is to treat each word as a node in an implicit graph where edges exist between words that differ by exactly one character. Starting from the beginWord, we perform DFS to explore all possible transformation sequences to reach the endWord, tracking the shortest path found. The algorithm works by recursively trying every possible one-character change at each step, checking if the new word exists in the word list and hasn't been visited in the current path. We maintain a global minimum to track the shortest sequence length. The time complexity is O(b^d) where b is the branching factor (average number of valid transformations per word) and d is the depth (transformation steps needed). Space complexity is O(d) for the recursion stack and visited set. This connects to L1 by using the transformation generation as a key subroutine. Common mistakes include not preventing cycles (revisiting words in same path), not properly resetting visited states during backtracking, and inefficient word existence checks. Real code pattern: def dfs(current, target, word_set, visited, path_length, min_length): if current == target: update min_length; for each possible transformation: if valid and not visited: mark visited, recurse, unmark visited.

examples.trace:
Start: hit (path length 1)
DFS hit → hot (length 2, visited: hit,hot)
  DFS hot → dot (length 3, visited: hit,hot,dot)
    DFS dot → dog (length 4, visited: hit,hot,dot,dog)
      DFS dog → cog (length 5, visited: hit,hot,dot,dog,cog) → Found! Update min_length=5
      Backtrack: remove cog from visited
    Backtrack: remove dog from visited
    DFS dot → lot (length 4, visited: hit,hot,dot,lot)
      DFS lot → log (length 5, visited: hit,hot,dot,lot,log)
        DFS log → cog (length 6, visited: hit,hot,dot,lot,log,cog) → Found! min_length remains 5
        Backtrack: remove cog
      Backtrack: remove log
    Backtrack: remove lot
  Backtrack: remove dot
  DFS hot → lot (length 3, visited: hit,hot,lot)
    DFS lot → dot (length 4, visited: hit,hot,lot,dot) → dot already visited in this path, skip
    DFS lot → log (length 4, visited: hit,hot,lot,log)
      DFS log → dog (length 5, visited: hit,hot,lot,log,dog)
        DFS dog → cog (length 6, visited: hit,hot,lot,log,dog,cog) → Found! min_length remains 5
        Backtrack: remove cog
      Backtrack: remove dog
      DFS log → cog (length 5, visited: hit,hot,lot,log,cog) → Found! min_length remains 5
      Backtrack: remove cog
    Backtrack: remove log
  Backtrack: remove lot
Backtrack: remove hot
Return min_length=5

Start: a (path length 1)
DFS a → b (length 2, visited: a,b)
  DFS b → c (length 3, visited: a,b,c) → Found! Update min_length=3
  Backtrack: remove c
Backtrack: remove b
DFS a → c (length 2, visited: a,c) → Found! Update min_length=2
Backtrack: remove c
Return min_length=2

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Generate All Valid One-Character Transformations
ladders.desc: Function that generates all words reachable by changing exactly one character of a given word.
ladders.explanation: This is the most important helper function for the DFS approach. The intuition is that at each step in the word ladder, we can only change one character, so we need to efficiently find all possible next words. For a given current word, we generate all strings that differ by exactly one character at any position, then filter to only those that exist in our word list. The algorithm works by iterating through each character position (0 to length-1), for each position trying all 26 possible letters (excluding the original letter), constructing the new word, and checking if it's in the word set. We return a list of all valid transformations. Time complexity is O(L × 26) = O(L) where L is word length, since we check 26 possibilities for each of L positions. Space complexity is O(K) where K is the number of valid transformations found. This feeds into L0 by providing the branching options at each DFS step. Common mistakes include not excluding the original letter when generating transformations (wasting cycles), not using a set for O(1) membership checks, and inefficiently regenerating the same transformations. Real code pattern: def get_neighbors(word, word_set): neighbors = []; for i in range(len(word)): for c in 'abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz': if c != word[i]: new_word = word[:i] + c + word[i+1:]; if new_word in word_set: neighbors.append(new_word); return neighbors.

examples.trace:
Word length: 3
Position 0 (h): try a-z except h
  'ait' not in set, 'bit' not in set, ... 'git' not in set, 'iit' not in set, ... 'zit' not in set
Position 1 (i): try a-z except i
  'hat' not in set, 'hbt' not in set, ... 'hht' not in set, 'hjt' not in set, ... 'hzt' not in set
  'hot' in set → add to neighbors
Position 2 (t): try a-z except t
  'hia' not in set, 'hib' not in set, ... 'hiz' not in set
Return ['hot']

Word length: 3
Position 0 (h): try a-z except h
  'aot' not in set, 'bot' not in set, ... 'iot' not in set, 'jot' not in set, ... 'zot' not in set
  'dot' in set → add to neighbors
  'lot' in set → add to neighbors
Position 1 (o): try a-z except o
  'hat' not in set, 'hbt' not in set, ... 'hht' not in set, 'hit' in set → add to neighbors, 'hjt' not in set, ... 'hzt' not in set
Position 2 (t): try a-z except t
  'hoa' not in set, 'hob' not in set, ... 'hoz' not in set
Return ['dot','lot','hit']

ladders.title: Core Logic — DFS with Backtracking for Path Exploration
ladders.desc: Depth-first search algorithm that explores all paths while tracking visited nodes and path length.
ladders.explanation: This is the core DFS logic that explores transformation sequences. The intuition is that we need to systematically try all possible paths from start to end, similar to exploring a maze where each word is a room and transformations are doors. DFS allows us to go deep into one path before backtracking to try alternatives. The algorithm maintains a visited set to prevent cycles within the current path (but allows reuse across different paths via backtracking), a current path length, and a global minimum. At each step, if we reach the target, we update the minimum. Otherwise, we generate all valid neighbors, and for each unvisited neighbor, we mark it visited, recursively explore, then unmark it (backtracking). Time complexity depends on branching factor and depth. Space complexity is O(d) for recursion stack and visited set. This connects to L1 by using the transformation generation to get neighbors. Common mistakes include not properly backtracking (leaving nodes marked visited), not comparing against a global minimum correctly, and not handling the case where no path exists. Real code pattern: def dfs(current, target, word_set, visited, path_length, min_length): if current == target: min_length[0] = min(min_length[0], path_length); return; for neighbor in get_neighbors(current, word_set): if neighbor not in visited: visited.add(neighbor); dfs(neighbor, target, word_set, visited, path_length+1, min_length); visited.remove(neighbor).

examples.input: start='a', end='c', word_set={'a','b','c'}, visited=set(), path_length=1, min_length=[inf]
examples.trace:
Current: 'a', target: 'c', path_length=1
'a' != 'c'
Get neighbors of 'a': ['b']
Neighbor 'b' not visited
  visited={'a','b'}, path_length=2
  DFS 'b':
    'b' != 'c'
    Get neighbors of 'b': ['a','c']
    Neighbor 'a' already visited, skip
    Neighbor 'c' not visited
      visited={'a','b','c'}, path_length=3
      DFS 'c':
        'c' == 'c' → update min_length to min(inf,3)=3
      Backtrack: remove 'c' from visited
  Backtrack: remove 'b' from visited
Get neighbors of 'a' also includes 'c' (from transformation generation)
Neighbor 'c' not visited
  visited={'a','c'}, path_length=2
  DFS 'c':
    'c' == 'c' → update min_length to min(3,2)=2
  Backtrack: remove 'c' from visited
Final min_length=[2]

Current: 'hit', target: 'cog', path_length=1
'hit' != 'cog'
Get neighbors of 'hit': ['hot']
Neighbor 'hot' not visited
  visited={'hit','hot'}, path_length=2
  DFS 'hot':
    'hot' != 'cog'
    Get neighbors of 'hot': ['dot','lot']
    Neighbor 'dot' not visited
      visited={'hit','hot','dot'}, path_length=3
      DFS 'dot':
        'dot' != 'cog'
        Get neighbors of 'dot': ['dog','lot']
        Neighbor 'dog' not visited
          visited={'hit','hot','dot','dog'}, path_length=4
          DFS 'dog':
            'dog' != 'cog'
            Get neighbors of 'dog': ['cog','log']
            Neighbor 'cog' not visited
              visited={'hit','hot','dot','dog','cog'}, path_length=5
              DFS 'cog': 'cog' == 'cog' → update min_length to 5
              Backtrack: remove 'cog'
            Neighbor 'log' not visited
              visited={'hit','hot','dot','dog','log'}, path_length=5
              DFS 'log':
                'log' != 'cog'
                Get neighbors of 'log': ['cog','dog']
                Neighbor 'cog' not visited
                  visited={'hit','hot','dot','dog','log','cog'}, path_length=6
                  DFS 'cog': update min_length to min(5,6)=5
                  Backtrack: remove 'cog'
                Neighbor 'dog' already visited, skip
              Backtrack: remove 'log'
          Backtrack: remove 'dog'
        Neighbor 'lot' not visited
          visited={'hit','hot','dot','lot'}, path_length=4
          DFS 'lot': (similar exploration, will find path of length 5 or 6)
          Backtrack: remove 'lot'
      Backtrack: remove 'dot'
    Neighbor 'lot' not visited
      visited={'hit','hot','lot'}, path_length=3
      DFS 'lot': (exploration similar to above)
      Backtrack: remove 'lot'
  Backtrack: remove 'hot'
Final min_length=[5]

ladders.title: Building Block — Set Operations for Efficient Membership Checking
ladders.desc: Using hash sets to check if a word exists in the word list in constant time.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is crucial for efficiency in the DFS approach. The intuition is that we need to repeatedly check if generated transformation words exist in our word list. If we store the word list in a list and do linear searches, each check would be O(N) where N is the list size, making the overall algorithm prohibitively slow. By converting the word list to a hash set, we achieve O(1) average time for membership checks. The algorithm simply creates a set from the given word list at the beginning, then uses the 'in' operator to check if a word exists. We also need to ensure the endWord is in the set before starting DFS, otherwise no path exists. Time complexity for set creation is O(N) where N is word list size, but this is a one-time cost. Space complexity is O(N) for storing the set. This connects to L2 by providing the word_set parameter needed for DFS. Common mistakes include not converting to a set (using list instead), not checking if endWord is in the set, and modifying the set during DFS (which should be avoided). Real code pattern: word_set = set(wordList); if endWord not in word_set: return 0;.

examples.trace:
Create set: {'hot','dot','dog','lot','log','cog'}
Check 'hot' in set: True
Check 'hat' in set: False
Check 'cog' in set: True
Check 'cat' in set: False

Create set: {'a','b','c','d','e'}
Check 'a' in set: True
Check 'f' in set: False
Check 'c' in set: True
Check 'z' in set: False

ladders.title: Basic Operation — String Manipulation for Character Replacement
ladders.desc: Creating a new string by changing one character at a specific position.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is used repeatedly in the transformation generation. The intuition is that to generate a possible transformation, we need to create a new string that is identical to the original except at one position where we change the character. Strings in Python are immutable, so we cannot modify them in place. Instead, we create a new string by concatenating three parts: the prefix (characters before the position), the new character, and the suffix (characters after the position). The algorithm takes the original string, an index position (0-based), and a new character. It returns a new string where the character at the given index is replaced with the new character. Time complexity is O(L) where L is string length, since we're creating a new string. Space complexity is O(L) for the new string. This connects to L3 by being the core operation used when generating transformation candidates. Common mistakes include using string indexing incorrectly (off-by-one errors), not handling edge cases (position at beginning or end), and inefficient string concatenation in loops. Real code pattern: def replace_char(word, index, new_char): return word[:index] + new_char + word[index+1:].

examples.trace:
word = 'hit', length=3
index=1, new_char='o'
Prefix: word[:1] = 'h'
Suffix: word[2:] = 't' (since index+1=2)
New string: 'h' + 'o' + 't' = 'hot'

word = 'game', length=4
index=0, new_char='f'
Prefix: word[:0] = '' (empty string)
Suffix: word[1:] = 'ame'
New string: '' + 'f' + 'ame' = 'fame'

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — String Equality and Comparison
ladders.desc: Understanding how to check if two strings are identical.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept needed for the word ladder problem. The intuition is that at the most basic level, we need to know when we've reached our destination - when the current word equals the end word. String comparison checks if two strings have exactly the same characters in the same order. The algorithm simply uses the equality operator (==) to compare two strings. It returns True if they are identical, False otherwise. Time complexity is O(L) where L is string length, since in worst case we need to compare all characters. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L4 as it's used to determine when to stop the DFS recursion. Common mistakes include using 'is' instead of '==' (which checks object identity, not value equality), not considering case sensitivity (if not specified, assume case matters), and not understanding that empty strings are valid strings. Real code pattern: if current_word == end_word: return True else: return False.

examples.trace:
Compare character by character:
Position 0: 'h' == 'h' ✓
Position 1: 'i' == 'i' ✓
Position 2: 't' == 't' ✓
All characters match → True

Compare character by character:
Position 0: 'h' == 'h' ✓
Position 1: 'o' != 'i' ✗
Found mismatch at position 1 → False

approaches.name: BFS Single Direction O(n * l * 26)
approaches.summary: Use breadth-first search from the start word, exploring all words that differ by one character and are in the word list, to find the shortest transformation sequence. This ensures the shortest path by level-order traversal.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Word Ladder I with BFS Single Direction
ladders.desc: Find the shortest transformation sequence from start to end word by changing one letter at a time, with all intermediate words in the given word list.
ladders.explanation: This problem models an unweighted shortest path search in an implicit graph where words are nodes and edges exist between words differing by exactly one character. The BFS single direction approach guarantees finding the shortest path by exploring words level by level from the start word. Intuition: BFS explores all possible transformations at the current distance before moving further, ensuring the first time we reach the end word is via the shortest sequence. Step-by-step: 1) Convert word list to a set for O(1) lookups. 2) Use a queue for BFS, initialized with (start_word, distance=1). 3) While queue not empty: pop word and distance; if word equals end, return distance. 4) For each position in word, try all 26 letters to generate neighbors; if neighbor in word set, add to queue with distance+1 and remove from set to prevent revisits. 5) If queue empties without finding end, return 0. Time: O(n * l * 26) where n is number of words, l is word length—each word processed once, each position tries 26 letters. Space: O(n) for queue and set. Connection to L1: This integrates the neighbor generation subroutine. Common mistakes: Not removing visited words from set (causes cycles), using list instead of set for word list (O(n) lookups), forgetting to handle case where end not in word list. Real code pattern: from collections import deque; wordSet = set(wordList); if endWord not in wordSet: return 0; queue = deque([(beginWord, 1)]); while queue: word, dist = queue.popleft(); if word == endWord: return dist; for i in range(len(word)): for c in 'abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz': next_word = word[:i] + c + word[i+1:]; if next_word in wordSet: queue.append((next_word, dist+1)); wordSet.remove(next_word); return 0.

examples.trace: Step 1: wordSet = {'hot','dot','dog','lot','log','cog'}, queue = [('hit',1)]. Step 2: Process 'hit' (dist=1): Generate neighbors: at position 0: 'ait','bit',...,'hot' (in set) -> add ('hot',2), remove 'hot' from set; others not in set. Queue: [('hot',2)]. Step 3: Process 'hot' (dist=2): Neighbors: 'dot','lot' (both in set) -> add ('dot',3) and ('lot',3), remove both from set. Queue: [('dot',3),('lot',3)]. Step 4: Process 'dot' (dist=3): Neighbors: 'dog' (in set) -> add ('dog',4), remove 'dog'. Queue: [('lot',3),('dog',4)]. Step 5: Process 'lot' (dist=3): Neighbors: 'log' (in set) -> add ('log',4), remove 'log'. Queue: [('dog',4),('log',4)]. Step 6: Process 'dog' (dist=4): Neighbors: 'cog' (in set) -> add ('cog',5), remove 'cog'. Queue: [('log',4),('cog',5)]. Step 7: Process 'log' (dist=4): Neighbors: 'cog' (not in set, removed). Queue: [('cog',5)]. Step 8: Process 'cog' (dist=5): word == endWord, return 5.

Step 1: wordSet = {'hot','dog'}, queue = [('hot',1)]. Step 2: Process 'hot' (dist=1): Generate neighbors: try all positions and letters; only 'dot','hog',etc., none in set except 'hot' itself (ignored). Queue becomes empty. Step 3: Return 0 as endWord not reached.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Generate All One-Letter-Different Neighbors
ladders.desc: Given a word, generate all possible words that differ by exactly one character and are in a given word set.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of BFS exploration in Word Ladder. Intuition: To find transformations, we need to efficiently discover valid next words from the current word by changing one letter at a time. By iterating through each position in the word and trying all 26 possible letters, we can generate all potential neighbors. Checking membership in a hash set ensures O(1) validation. Step-by-step: 1) Input: current word, word set. 2) For each index i from 0 to len(word)-1: save original character; for each letter c in 'a' to 'z': if c != original, form new word by replacing character at i with c; if new word in word set, collect it. 3) Return list of valid neighbors. Time: O(l * 26) per word, where l is word length—constant per word. Space: O(1) extra space for generation. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called for each word processed in BFS to expand the frontier. Common mistakes: Not excluding the original word itself (wasteful), using list for word set (slow lookups), not handling uppercase (convert to lowercase first). Real code pattern: def get_neighbors(word, word_set): neighbors = []; for i in range(len(word)): for c in 'abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz': if c != word[i]: next_word = word[:i] + c + word[i+1:]; if next_word in word_set: neighbors.append(next_word); return neighbors.

examples.trace: Step 1: For i=0 (letter 'h'): try 'aot','bot',...,'dot' (in set) -> add 'dot'; 'lot' not at this position. Continue through letters; no others in set. Step 2: For i=1 (letter 'o'): try 'hat','hbt',...,'hlt' (none in set). Step 3: For i=2 (letter 't'): try 'hoa','hob',...,'hol' -> 'hol' not in set, but 'lot' not generated here. Actually, 'lot' is generated at i=0 with letter 'l'? Wait, re-check: At i=0, letter 'l' gives 'lot' which is in set -> add 'lot'. So neighbors: 'dot' and 'lot'.

Step 1: i=0: try letters: 'a'->'aat' not in set, 'b'->'bat' in set -> add 'bat', 'c'->original skip, 'd' to 'z' none in set. Step 2: i=1: try letters: 'a'->'cat' original skip, 'b'->'cbt' not, 'o'->'cot' in set -> add 'cot', others none. Step 3: i=2: try letters: 'a'->'caa' not, 'r'->'car' in set -> add 'car', 't' original skip, 'm'->'cam' not, but 'mat'? 'mat' requires changing 'c' to 'm' at i=0, so at i=0, letter 'm' gives 'mat' in set -> add 'mat'. So all four found.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Breadth-First Search for Shortest Path in Implicit Graph
ladders.desc: Use BFS to find the shortest path between two nodes in a graph where edges are defined implicitly by one-letter differences.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the BFS algorithm itself, abstracted from the word ladder context. Intuition: BFS is ideal for unweighted graphs because it explores all nodes at the current distance before moving to the next, guaranteeing that the first time we reach the target node, it's via the shortest path. In an implicit graph, we don't have a pre-built adjacency list; instead, we generate neighbors on-the-fly using a rule (one-letter change). Step-by-step: 1) Initialize a queue with start node and distance 0. 2) Use a visited set to avoid cycles. 3) While queue not empty: dequeue node and distance; if node is target, return distance. 4) Generate all neighbors of node using the implicit rule. 5) For each neighbor not visited: mark visited, enqueue with distance+1. 6) If queue empties, target unreachable. Time: O(V + E) in explicit graphs, but here depends on neighbor generation; in Word Ladder, O(n * l * 26). Space: O(V) for queue and visited. Connection to L1: BFS uses the neighbor generation subroutine to expand nodes. Common mistakes: Using DFS instead (won't guarantee shortest path), not tracking distance per node, not marking visited before enqueueing (causes duplicates). Real code pattern: from collections import deque; def bfs_shortest_path(start, target, get_neighbors_func): if start == target: return 0; queue = deque([(start, 0)]); visited = {start}; while queue: node, dist = queue.popleft(); for neighbor in get_neighbors_func(node): if neighbor == target: return dist+1; if neighbor not in visited: visited.add(neighbor); queue.append((neighbor, dist+1)); return -1.

examples.input: start = 'A', target = 'D', get_neighbors_func returns neighbors: A->[B,C], B->[D], C->[D]
examples.trace: Step 1: queue = [('A',0)], visited = {'A'}. Step 2: Dequeue 'A' dist=0; neighbors: 'B','C'. For 'B': not target, not visited -> visited add 'B', queue append ('B',1). For 'C': similar -> queue [('B',1),('C',1)]. Step 3: Dequeue 'B' dist=1; neighbor 'D' is target -> return dist+1 = 2.

examples.input: start = 'X', target = 'Y', get_neighbors_func returns: X->[Z], Z->[X]
examples.trace: Step 1: queue = [('X',0)], visited = {'X'}. Step 2: Dequeue 'X' dist=0; neighbor 'Z' not target, not visited -> visited add 'Z', queue append ('Z',1). Step 3: Dequeue 'Z' dist=1; neighbor 'X' visited, skip. Queue empty, return -1.

testCases.input: start = 'S', target = 'G', neighbors: S->[A,B], A->[C], B->[D], C->[G], D->[G]
start = 'P', target = 'Q', neighbors: P->[R], R->[S], S->[P]
start = 'A', target = 'E', neighbors: A->[B], B->[C], C->[D], D->[E]
start = 'L', target = 'M', neighbors: L->[N], N->[O], O->[P]

ladders.title: Building Block — Queue Operations for Level-Order Traversal
ladders.desc: Implement and use a queue to process nodes in first-in-first-out order, essential for BFS.
ladders.explanation: A queue is a fundamental data structure that follows FIFO (First-In-First-Out) order, which is crucial for BFS to ensure we explore nodes level by level. Intuition: In BFS, we want to visit all neighbors of the current node before moving to neighbors of neighbors; a queue naturally enforces this by adding new nodes to the back and processing from the front. Step-by-step operations: 1) Enqueue: add an element to the rear. 2) Dequeue: remove and return the front element. 3) Peek: view front without removal. 4) Check if empty. In Python, use collections.deque for efficient O(1) operations. For BFS, we enqueue start node, then repeatedly dequeue a node, process it, and enqueue its unvisited neighbors. Time: O(1) per enqueue/dequeue. Space: O(n) for storing nodes in queue. Connection to L2: The queue manages the frontier of nodes to explore in BFS. Common mistakes: Using a list with pop(0) (O(n) time), not handling empty queue, mixing up enqueue/dequeue order. Real code pattern: from collections import deque; q = deque(); q.append('start') # enqueue; while q: node = q.popleft() # dequeue; process node; q.append(neighbor) for each neighbor.

examples.input: Enqueue: A, B, C; then dequeue twice; then enqueue D, E; dequeue until empty.
examples.output: Dequeue order: A, B, C, D, E
examples.trace: Step 1: Queue = []; enqueue A -> [A]; enqueue B -> [A,B]; enqueue C -> [A,B,C]. Step 2: Dequeue -> returns A, queue = [B,C]; dequeue -> returns B, queue = [C]. Step 3: Enqueue D -> [C,D]; enqueue E -> [C,D,E]. Step 4: Dequeue -> returns C, queue = [D,E]; dequeue -> returns D, queue = [E]; dequeue -> returns E, queue = [].

examples.input: Start queue with (1,0); enqueue (2,1), (3,1); dequeue; enqueue (4,2); dequeue all.
examples.trace: Step 1: Queue = deque([(1,0)]). Step 2: Enqueue (2,1) -> [(1,0),(2,1)]; enqueue (3,1) -> [(1,0),(2,1),(3,1)]. Step 3: Dequeue -> returns (1,0), queue = [(2,1),(3,1)]. Step 4: Enqueue (4,2) -> [(2,1),(3,1),(4,2)]. Step 5: Dequeue -> returns (2,1), queue = [(3,1),(4,2)]; dequeue -> returns (3,1), queue = [(4,2)]; dequeue -> returns (4,2), queue = [].

testCases.input: Enqueue: 5,3,7; dequeue 2 times; output front element.
Enqueue: 'apple','banana'; dequeue; enqueue 'cherry'; dequeue all; output order.
Start empty; enqueue 10; dequeue; enqueue 20; dequeue; output queue size.
Queue: [1,2,3]; dequeue; enqueue 4; dequeue; output dequeued elements in order.
Enqueue pairs: (A,1),(B,2); dequeue; enqueue (C,3); dequeue all; output second elements.
Queue with 0 elements; attempt dequeue; output error or None.
Enqueue: X,Y,Z; dequeue 3 times; enqueue W; output front.
Start: [5]; dequeue; enqueue 6,7; dequeue; output dequeued value.
Enqueue: 100,200; dequeue; output queue contents as list.
Enqueue: 'start',0; dequeue; enqueue 'next',1; dequeue; output distance of second dequeued.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — String Manipulation: Replace Character at Index
ladders.desc: Create a new string by changing one character at a specified position, fundamental for generating word neighbors.
ladders.explanation: In Word Ladder, to generate neighbors, we need to modify a string by replacing a single character at a given index. Since strings are immutable in Python, we cannot change them in place; instead, we create a new string. Intuition: By slicing the original string into parts before and after the target index, we can insert a new character between them. Step-by-step: 1) Given string s, index i (0-based), and new character c. 2) Create left part: s[0:i] (characters from start up to but not including i). 3) Create right part: s[i+1:] (characters after i). 4) Concatenate left + c + right to form new string. Time: O(n) where n is string length, due to slicing and concatenation creating new strings. Space: O(n) for the new string. Connection to L1: This operation is used repeatedly in the neighbor generation subroutine. Common mistakes: Using index out of range, not handling empty strings, inefficient concatenation in loops (use list and join if many modifications). Real code pattern: def replace_char(s, i, c): return s[:i] + c + s[i+1:]; Example: replace_char('hot', 1, 'a') -> 'hat'.

examples.trace: Step 1: s[0:1] = 'h' (characters before index 1). Step 2: s[2:] = 'llo' (characters after index 1). Step 3: Concatenate: 'h' + 'a' + 'llo' = 'hallo'.
Step 1: s[0:3] = 'tes' (before index 3). Step 2: s[4:] = '' (after index 3, empty). Step 3: Concatenate: 'tes' + 'r' + '' = 'tesr'.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Sets for Fast Membership Testing
ladders.desc: Use a set data structure to store unique elements and check if an element exists in O(1) average time.
ladders.explanation: A set is a collection of unique elements that supports fast membership testing, which is crucial in Word Ladder to quickly check if a generated word is in the word list. Intuition: Unlike lists or arrays where checking membership requires O(n) time, sets use hash tables to achieve O(1) average time for lookups, making them efficient for frequent checks. Step-by-step: 1) Create a set from a list: word_set = set(wordList). 2) Check membership: if word in word_set returns True or False. 3) Add element: word_set.add(word). 4) Remove element: word_set.remove(word) or discard(word). In Word Ladder, we convert the word list to a set to speed up neighbor validation and remove visited words to prevent cycles. Time: O(1) for add, remove, and membership on average. Space: O(n) to store n elements. Connection to L4: Sets are used to store words, and string manipulation generates words to check against the set. Common mistakes: Using list for membership (slow), not handling duplicates (sets automatically handle uniqueness), confusing set with dictionary. Real code pattern: wordList = ['hot','dot','dog']; wordSet = set(wordList); print('hot' in wordSet) # True; wordSet.add('lot'); wordSet.remove('hot').

examples.trace: Step 1: Create set: {'cat','dog','fish'}. Step 2: Check 'dog' in set -> True. Step 3: Check 'bird' in set -> False.

examples.input: Start set = {1,2,3}; add 4; remove 2; check 2 and 4.
examples.trace: Step 1: Initial set = {1,2,3}. Step 2: Add 4 -> {1,2,3,4}. Step 3: Remove 2 -> {1,3,4}. Step 4: Check 2 in set -> False. Step 5: Check 4 in set -> True.

testCases.input: set = {100}; clear set; check if empty

approaches.name: Bidirectional BFS O(n * l * 26)
approaches.summary: Perform BFS simultaneously from both the start and end words, meeting in the middle to reduce the search space and speed up finding the shortest path. This cuts down the branching factor compared to single-direction BFS.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Bidirectional BFS for Word Ladder I
ladders.desc: Complete solution using bidirectional BFS to find shortest transformation sequence between two words.
ladders.explanation: The bidirectional BFS approach solves the word ladder problem by simultaneously exploring from both the start and end words, meeting in the middle. This dramatically reduces the search space compared to single-direction BFS because we're exploring two frontiers that grow exponentially—meeting in the middle means we only need to explore roughly half the depth from each side. The intuition is that BFS explores nodes level by level, and by starting from both ends, we can find the meeting point faster. The algorithm maintains two queues and two visited dictionaries (one for each direction). At each step, we expand the smaller frontier to balance the search. We generate all possible one-letter transformations of the current word and check if they exist in the word list and haven't been visited in that direction. When we find a word that has been visited from the opposite direction, we've found a meeting point and can calculate the total path length. Time complexity is O(n * l * 26) where n is number of words and l is word length—we might check all words and for each word generate all possible one-letter changes. Space complexity is O(n) for storing visited words and queues. This connects to L1 by using the word transformation generation as a key subroutine. Common mistakes include not handling the case where start and end words are the same, not properly tracking visited words separately for each direction, and not balancing which frontier to expand. Real code pattern: Initialize two queues with start and end words, two visited dictionaries, level counters for each direction. While both queues have elements, expand smaller frontier. For each word in current level, generate all one-letter transformations. If transformation is in word list and not visited in current direction, mark visited and add to queue. If transformation is visited in opposite direction, return sum of levels + 1.

examples.trace:
Start: hit (level 0 from start), cog (level 0 from end).
Step 1: Expand start frontier (smaller). From hit: generate transformations -> *it (none), h*t -> hot (in list). Add hot to start queue, mark visited_start[hot]=1.
Step 2: Expand end frontier (now smaller). From cog: generate -> *og (log, cog), c*g (cog), co* (cog). log is in list, not visited_end. Add log to end queue, mark visited_end[log]=1.
Step 3: Expand start frontier. From hot: generate -> *ot (dot, lot, hot), h*t (hit, hot), ho* (hot). dot and lot are in list, not visited_start. Add dot, lot to start queue, mark visited_start[dot]=2, visited_start[lot]=2.
Step 4: Expand end frontier. From log: generate -> *og (log, cog), l*g (log), lo* (log). dog is transformation (l*g -> dog). dog in list, not visited_end. Add dog to end queue, mark visited_end[dog]=2.
Step 5: Expand start frontier. From dot: generate -> *ot (dot, lot, hot), d*t (dot), do* (dog). dog is visited_end! Meeting found. start_level=2 (dot), end_level=2 (dog). Total = 2+2+1=5. Path: hit->hot->dot->dog->cog.

Start: talk (level 0 start), tail (level 0 end).
Step 1: Expand start frontier. From talk: generate -> *alk (walk, talk), t*lk (talk), ta*k (talk), tal* (tall). tall in list, not visited_start. Add tall to start queue, mark visited_start[tall]=1.
Step 2: Expand end frontier. From tail: generate -> *ail (tail, wait), t*il (tail), ta*l (tall, tail), tai* (tail). tall is visited_start! Meeting found. start_level=1 (tall), end_level=0 (tail). Total = 1+0+1=2. Path: talk->tall->tail.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Generating All One-Letter Transformations
ladders.desc: Generate all possible words by changing exactly one letter in a given word.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core operation that drives the BFS expansion in the word ladder problem. Given a word of length l, we need to generate all possible words that differ by exactly one letter. The intuition is that in the word ladder graph, each word is connected to all other words that are one letter different. By generating these transformations, we're essentially finding all neighbors of the current node in the implicit graph. The algorithm works by iterating through each position in the word (0 to l-1), and for each position, replacing that character with all 26 possible lowercase letters (a-z), creating new candidate words. We skip the original character itself to avoid generating the same word. Time complexity is O(l * 26) per word, where l is word length. Space complexity is O(l * 26) for storing the generated transformations, though we typically process them one at a time. This connects to L0 by being the operation performed at each BFS expansion step. Common mistakes include not handling uppercase letters (should convert to lowercase), not skipping the original character (wasting time), and not considering that the word list might contain duplicates. Real code pattern: For i in range(len(word)): For char in 'abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz': If char != word[i]: new_word = word[:i] + char + word[i+1:]; yield new_word.

examples.trace: Word length: 3. Position 0: Replace 'c' with a-z -> 'aat','bat',...,'zat' (26 words, but exclude 'cat' when char='c'). Position 1: Replace 'a' with a-z -> 'cbt','cct',...,'czt' (26 words, exclude 'cat' when char='a'). Position 2: Replace 't' with a-z -> 'caa','cab',...,'caz' (26 words, exclude 'cat' when char='t'). Total: 26*3 - 3 = 75 transformations (since we exclude the original word at each position).
Word length: 1. Position 0: Replace 'a' with a-z -> 'b','c',...,'z' (25 words, exclude 'a' itself). Total: 25 transformations.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Bidirectional BFS Meeting Point Detection
ladders.desc: Detect when two BFS frontiers meet and calculate the total path length.
ladders.explanation: The core insight of bidirectional BFS is that we search from both start and end simultaneously, and the algorithm terminates when we find a word that has been visited from both directions. This meeting point represents the midpoint of the shortest path. The intuition is that BFS explores nodes in layers (by distance from the source), so when we find a node visited from both sides, the sum of distances from each side gives the total path length (plus 1 for the meeting node itself). We maintain two separate visited dictionaries: one for nodes reached from the start, and one for nodes reached from the end. Each dictionary maps words to their distance from that source. When we expand a node from one direction, we check if any of its neighbors exist in the opposite direction's visited dictionary. If found, we calculate total length as distance_from_start + distance_from_end + 1. The '+1' accounts for the meeting node itself being counted in both distances. Time complexity for this detection is O(1) per neighbor check. Space complexity is O(n) for the visited dictionaries. This connects to L0 as the termination condition and result calculation. Common mistakes include forgetting to add 1 for the meeting node, not checking both directions properly, and not handling the case where start and end are the same word (should return 1 immediately). Real code pattern: When processing a word from start BFS: For each neighbor: If neighbor in visited_end: return visited_start[current] + visited_end[neighbor] + 1. Similarly for end BFS.

examples.trace: Current word 'hot' is from start BFS with distance 1. Its neighbor 'log' is found in visited_end with distance 1. Total path length = distance_from_start(hot) + distance_from_end(log) + 1 = 1 + 1 + 1 = 3. This represents path: hit->hot->log->cog (but note: 'log' might not directly connect to 'cog' in this example).
Current word 'tall' from start with distance 1. Neighbor 'tail' found in visited_end with distance 0. Total = 1 + 0 + 1 = 2. Path: talk->tall->tail.

ladders.title: Building Block — BFS Level-by-Level Expansion
ladders.desc: Perform BFS expansion one level at a time, tracking distances.
ladders.explanation: Breadth-First Search (BFS) explores nodes in layers based on their distance from the source. This building block implements the core BFS mechanism: starting from a source node, we visit all nodes at distance 1, then all nodes at distance 2, and so on. The intuition is that BFS guarantees we find the shortest path in an unweighted graph because we explore closer nodes before farther ones. We use a queue (FIFO) to maintain the frontier of nodes to explore. We also maintain a visited dictionary that maps nodes to their distance from the source. The algorithm: Initialize queue with source node, visited[source] = 0. While queue not empty: Process all nodes at current level (using level size). For each node, generate neighbors. For each neighbor not visited: mark visited with current distance + 1, add to queue. Time complexity is O(V + E) for a graph with V vertices and E edges. In word ladder, V is number of words, E is implicit connections. Space complexity is O(V) for queue and visited dictionary. This connects to L2 by being the mechanism that builds the visited dictionaries used for meeting point detection. Common mistakes include not tracking levels properly (mixing nodes from different levels), not marking nodes as visited when adding to queue (causing duplicates), and using DFS instead of BFS. Real code pattern: queue = deque([start]); visited = {start: 0}; while queue: level_size = len(queue); for _ in range(level_size): current = queue.popleft(); for neighbor in get_neighbors(current): if neighbor not in visited: visited[neighbor] = visited[current] + 1; queue.append(neighbor).

examples.input: start: 'A', neighbors: A->[B,C], B->[D], C->[E], D->[], E->[]
examples.trace:
Level 0: Queue: ['A'], visited['A']=0.
Process level 0 (size 1): Current='A'. Neighbors: B, C. Both not visited. visited['B']=1, visited['C']=1. Queue becomes ['B','C'].
Level 1: Process all nodes at this level (size 2). Current='B': Neighbor D not visited, visited['D']=2, queue add D. Current='C': Neighbor E not visited, visited['E']=2, queue add E. Queue now ['D','E'].
Level 2: Process all nodes (size 2). Current='D': No unvisited neighbors. Current='E': No unvisited neighbors. Queue empty. Done.

Start: 'cat' (level 0). Generate neighbors: 'bat' (c->b), 'cap' (t->p), 'cot' (a->o). All in wordList. Level 0: Process 'cat'. Add neighbors to queue with distance 1. Queue: ['bat','cap','cot']. Level 1: Process all. 'bat': neighbors might include 'cat' (visited), 'cap' (not in list), etc. No new unvisited in wordList. 'cap': neighbors include 'cat' (visited), 'tap' (not in list). 'cot': neighbors include 'cat' (visited), 'dot' (not in list). Queue empty. Note: 'dog' not reachable with one-letter changes from these nodes.

testCases.input: start: 'A', graph: {A:[B], B:[C], C:[D], D:[E], E:[]}
start: 'root', graph: {root:[child1,child2], child1:[grand1], child2:[grand2], grand1:[], grand2:[]}

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Queue Management for BFS
ladders.desc: Implement FIFO queue operations for BFS traversal.
ladders.explanation: A queue is a fundamental data structure that follows First-In-First-Out (FIFO) order, which is essential for BFS. In BFS, we need to process nodes in the order they were discovered: the first node discovered should be the first one expanded. The intuition is that this ensures we explore all nodes at distance d before any nodes at distance d+1, which is what gives BFS its shortest-path property. Basic operations include enqueue (add to back), dequeue (remove from front), peek (view front without removing), and check if empty. For BFS, we typically use a deque (double-ended queue) for efficient operations at both ends. Time complexity for enqueue and dequeue is O(1). Space complexity is O(n) where n is number of elements in queue. This connects to L3 as the data structure that enables level-by-level expansion. Common mistakes include using a list with pop(0) which is O(n), not checking if queue is empty before dequeue, and confusing queue with stack (LIFO). Real code pattern: from collections import deque; queue = deque(); queue.append(item) # enqueue; item = queue.popleft() # dequeue; if not queue: # empty check.

examples.trace:
Start: empty queue [].
Enqueue 'A': queue becomes ['A'] (front='A', back='A').
Enqueue 'B': queue becomes ['A','B'] (front='A', back='B').
Enqueue 'C': queue becomes ['A','B','C'] (front='A', back='C').
Dequeue: Remove front 'A', queue becomes ['B','C'] (front='B', back='C'). Return 'A'.
Dequeue: Remove front 'B', queue becomes ['C'] (front='C', back='C'). Return 'B'.

Queue: [] -> enqueue 1: [1] -> enqueue 2: [1,2] -> enqueue 3: [1,2,3].
Dequeue: remove 1, queue [2,3], return 1.
Dequeue: remove 2, queue [3], return 2.
Dequeue: remove 3, queue [], return 3.
Queue empty.

testCases.input: Operations: enqueue('x'), enqueue('y'), dequeue(), enqueue('z'), dequeue(), dequeue()
Start empty, enqueue 5, enqueue 10, dequeue, enqueue 15, dequeue, dequeue
Enqueue 'alpha', 'beta', 'gamma', dequeue, dequeue, enqueue 'delta', dequeue, dequeue
Enqueue 'first', enqueue 'second', dequeue, enqueue 'third', dequeue, dequeue
Enqueue 100,200,300, dequeue, enqueue 400, dequeue, dequeue, dequeue
Enqueue 'start', dequeue, enqueue 'mid', dequeue, enqueue 'end', dequeue

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graph as Set of Connected Nodes
ladders.desc: Understand the basic concept of a graph as nodes and edges.
ladders.explanation: A graph is a fundamental data structure consisting of nodes (also called vertices) and edges connecting pairs of nodes. In the context of word ladder, each word is a node, and an edge exists between two words if they differ by exactly one letter. This forms an implicit graph—we don't have the graph stored explicitly, but we can generate neighbors on the fly. The intuition is that many problems can be modeled as graphs where we need to find paths between nodes. Key concepts: nodes represent entities, edges represent relationships, a path is a sequence of connected nodes, and the shortest path is the path with the fewest edges. Time and space considerations depend on how the graph is represented (adjacency list, matrix, etc.). This connects to L4 because BFS is an algorithm for traversing graphs. Common mistakes include confusing directed vs undirected graphs (word ladder is undirected—if A connects to B, then B connects to A), not handling disconnected graphs, and not understanding that graphs can have cycles. Real code pattern: Graph can be represented as dictionary: graph = {'hit': ['hot'], 'hot': ['hit','dot','lot'], ...} but in word ladder we generate neighbors dynamically.

examples.output: Graph: cat connected to bat, bat connected to cat and rat, rat connected to bat
examples.trace: Nodes: 'cat', 'bat', 'rat'. Check pairs: 'cat' and 'bat' differ by first letter (c vs b) -> edge. 'cat' and 'rat' differ by first letter (c vs r) -> edge. 'bat' and 'rat' differ by first letter (b vs r) -> edge. So graph: cat-bat, cat-rat, bat-rat. All connected.

Nodes: 'hot', 'dot', 'dog'. Check: 'hot' and 'dot' differ by first letter (h vs d) -> edge. 'hot' and 'dog' differ by first and third letters -> no edge. 'dot' and 'dog' differ by third letter (t vs g) -> edge. So graph: hot connected to dot, dot connected to hot and dog, dog connected to dot.

testCases.expected: Graph: A connected to B, B connected to A and C, C connected to B
Graph: red-bed, bed-red and bed-bet, bet-bed
Graph: cat-cot, cot-cat and cot-cog, cog-cot and cog-dog, dog-cog
Graph: single node 'same' with no edges (same word doesn't connect to itself)
Graph: a-b, a-c, b-a and b-c, c-a and c-b
Graph: word-work, work-word and work-fork, fork-work and fork-form, form-fork
Graph: start-stark, stark-start and stark-stack, stack-stark and stack-slack, slack-stack
Graph: node1 connected to node2 and node3, node2 connected to node1 and node3, node3 connected to node1 and node2
